metadata.summary: Manual AT testing (NVDA, JAWS, VoiceOver, TalkBack) catches 50-70% of a11y issues automated tools miss. Keyboard navigation, screen reader checks, smoke tests to comprehensive audits.

content.title: Testing with Assistive Technology
content.summary: Manual testing with real screen readers, keyboard-only navigation, and mobile AT to catch the 50-70% of accessibility issues that automated tools miss. Covers NVDA, JAWS, VoiceOver (macOS/iOS), and TalkBack (Android), with tiered test workflows from 15-minute smoke checks to 4-hour comprehensive audits.

why.p: Automated scanners (axe, WAVE, Lighthouse) detect ~30-50% of WCAG violations. The remainder — focus management bugs, incorrect live-region timing, custom-widget semantics, and misleading alt text — only surface when a real AT processes the DOM and produces speech output. AT + browser pairings matter and diverge; passing one combo does not generalize.

list.item: Verifying screen-reader and keyboard parity before any web, iOS, Android, or Electron release.
Catching focus-management regressions (modal close, route transitions, toast announcements).
Reproducing user-reported AT bugs with the exact screen reader + browser combo they use.
Authoring Gherkin test cases that a Guidepup agent or human tester can execute.
Pre-release sign-off on custom widgets (combobox, tree, grid, live regions).
First-pass scanning — run `a11y-testing` with axe/Pa11y first to remove easy issues.
Compliance paperwork / VPAT generation — use `regulatory-compliance-2026` with AT findings as input.
Native mobile UI design reviews (not runtime AT) — those are design-phase concerns.
Fully automated AT-driven E2E without human verification — screen-reader automation reads the accessibility tree, not real speech; results lie.

rule.statement: Test with the AT + browser pairs that real users actually use: NVDA+Firefox, JAWS+Chrome, VoiceOver+Safari, TalkBack+Chrome Android.
rule.rationale: Screen readers and browsers diverge on ARIA interpretation; a green run on one combo does not generalize to another.

section.name: nvda-windows
section.p: Free, ~40% market share. Download from nvaccess.org. Pair with Firefox for best standards coverage.

example.title: NVDA Key Commands
example.code: NVDA modifier: Insert or Caps Lock Stop speech: Ctrl Navigate headings: H (next) / Shift+H (prev) Navigate links: K / Shift+K Navigate form fields: F / Shift+F Forms mode: automatic on form entry Read all: NVDA + Down Arrow Landmarks: D / Shift+D
example.note: Start NVDA before the browser; restart if speech stalls.

section.name: jaws-windows
section.p: Paid ($95-1500), ~40% market share. Enterprise baseline. Pair with Chrome or Edge.

example.title: JAWS Key Commands
example.code: JAWS modifier: Caps Lock or Insert Stop speech: Ctrl Virtual cursor: Arrow keys Forms mode: Enter/Space on form element Read all: Insert + Down Arrow Headings: H / Shift+H Links: Tab / U (unvisited links)

section.name: voiceover-apple
section.p: Built-in, ~15% desktop market share; leading on iOS. Pair with Safari.

example.title: VoiceOver Key Commands
example.code: macOS activation: Cmd + F5 iOS activation: triple-click Home/Side button VO modifier: Ctrl + Option (abbreviated VO) Navigate: VO + Right/Left Arrow Interact: VO + Shift + Down Arrow Exit interaction: VO + Shift + Up Arrow Rotor (web nav): VO + U

section.name: talkback-android
section.p: Built-in Android default. Pair with Chrome. Gestures are model- and OS-version-specific.

example.title: TalkBack Gestures
example.code: Activation: Volume up + down simultaneously Navigate: Swipe right/left Activate: Double tap Context menu: Swipe down then right (L shape) Reading controls: Swipe up/down

section.name: automation-boundary

rule.statement: Distinguish accessibility-tree probes from real speech output in every test report.
rule.rationale: Most "AT automation" (axe-core, Guidepup tree mode) reads the OS accessibility tree, not actual NVDA/VoiceOver speech. Bugs where the tree is correct but the SR announces nothing are invisible to tree-only tools.

section.p: Real-speech automation: Guidepup driving NVDA on Windows (JS), voiceover-test driving VO via AppleScript (macOS). Both are slow and platform-locked — use for a curated critical-flow subset only.

rule.statement: Every interactive element must be reachable and operable using only Tab, Shift+Tab, Enter, Space, Escape, and Arrow keys — no mouse required.
rule.rationale: Screen reader users navigate by keyboard; motor-disability users may not use a mouse; legal requirement under WCAG 2.1 SC 2.1.1.

example.title: Core Keyboard Commands
example.code: Tab: move to next focusable element Shift+Tab: move to previous focusable element Enter: activate link or button Space: activate button / toggle checkbox Arrow keys: navigate dropdown options, radio groups, tabs, sliders Escape: close modal / cancel action

rule.statement: Never remove the focus outline without providing a visible replacement with at least 3:1 contrast ratio.
rule.rationale: WCAG 2.2 SC 2.4.11 (AA): focused element must be at least partially visible. SC 2.4.13 (AAA): 2px minimum thickness, sufficient contrast.

antipattern.description: CSS rule outline: none or outline: 0 applied globally without a replacement focus style.
antipattern.reason: Keyboard users cannot see where they are; fails WCAG 2.4.7 and 2.4.11.

rule.statement: Custom interactive components must implement the keyboard interaction pattern specified in the ARIA Authoring Practices Guide (APG).
rule.rationale: Arbitrary implementations break AT user expectations built over years of using native widgets.

example.title: Component Keyboard Patterns
example.code: Accordion: Enter/Space to expand/collapse; Tab to next control Tabs: Arrow keys to switch tabs; Tab to move into tab panel Modal: Tab cycles within modal; Escape closes; focus returns to trigger Carousel: Arrow keys for prev/next; focus managed per panel Dropdown: Enter/Space to open; Arrow keys to navigate; Escape to close Slider: Arrow keys to adjust value; Home/End for min/max

antipattern.description: Drag-and-drop with no keyboard alternative (e.g., reorderable list, Kanban board).
antipattern.reason: Fails WCAG 2.5.7 (AA, new in 2.2). Must provide button-driven move alternatives for every drag interaction.

list.item: No visible focus — outline removed without replacement.
Illogical tab order — DOM order differs from visual layout; wrong tabindex values.
Keyboard trap — cannot Tab out of a widget or modal.
Mouse-only hover menus — no keyboard trigger to open navigation flyouts.
Focus lost after modal close — not returned to the trigger element.
Enter/Space not handled on custom div/span buttons.

rule.statement: Every image must have a meaningful alt attribute; decorative images must have alt="" so they are skipped.
rule.rationale: WCAG 1.1.1 Non-text Content (Level A). A missing or generic alt (e.g., "image.jpg") conveys nothing to SR users.

list.item: All images have alt text.
Alt text is meaningful, not generic ("photo", "icon").
Decorative images have alt="" and no ARIA label.
Complex images (charts, diagrams) have long descriptions via figcaption or aria-describedby.
Inline SVG icons have aria-label or title + role="img".

rule.statement: Headings must form a logical hierarchy (H1 → H2 → H3) with no skipped levels; landmarks must mark major regions.
rule.rationale: SR users navigate by heading (H key in NVDA/JAWS) and by landmark (D key). Missing or skipped headings force linear reading of entire pages.

list.item: One H1 per page.
Headings hierarchical — no skipped levels (e.g., H1 → H3).
Landmarks present: main, nav, header, footer.
Lists marked up as ul/ol/dl, not styled divs.
Data tables have th elements with scope; caption provided.
Reading order matches visual order.

rule.statement: Every form input must have a programmatically associated label; error messages must be announced via ARIA live region or focus management.
rule.rationale: WCAG 1.3.1, 3.3.1, 3.3.2. "Edit, blank" is the most common screen reader announcement — it means no label is associated.

list.item: All inputs have associated label for="..." or aria-label / aria-labelledby.
Error messages linked to inputs via aria-describedby.
Required fields marked with aria-required="true" or required attribute.
Helper/hint text associated with inputs via aria-describedby.
Field groups wrapped in fieldset + legend.
Form validation errors announced — via live region or focus shift to error summary.

rule.statement: All asynchronous state changes (loading, success, error, count updates) must be announced via an appropriate ARIA live region.
rule.rationale: SR users receive no visual feedback from DOM mutations unless the accessibility tree signals a change.

list.item: aria-live="polite" for non-critical updates (search results count, auto-save status).
aria-live="assertive" for critical errors only — interrupts current SR reading.
Loading spinners have aria-live or aria-busy="true" on the container.
Modal dialogs trap focus on open; focus returns to trigger on close.
Modals have role="dialog", aria-modal="true", and aria-labelledby pointing to title.
Skip link to main content is first focusable element; visible on focus.
All links have descriptive text — no bare "click here" or "read more".
Current page indicated with aria-current="page" on nav link.
Link purpose is clear from link text alone or link text + programmatic context.

rule.statement: Run a 15-minute smoke test on every PR; reserve the comprehensive 2-4 hour pass for release candidates and major feature launches.
rule.rationale: Full AT testing is expensive; catching regressions early on targeted flows prevents compound debt without blocking velocity.

example.title: 15-Minute Smoke Test
example.code:
1. Keyboard pass (5 min) - Tab through entire page - All interactive elements reachable? - Focus indicator always visible? - No keyboard traps?
2. Screen reader spot check (5 min) - Navigate headings (H key in NVDA) - Navigate landmarks (D key in NVDA) - Try one form field — label announced?
3. Automated scan (5 min) - Run axe DevTools - Zero critical violations?

example.title: 2-4 Hour Comprehensive Test
example.code: 1. Automated scan (30 min): axe + WAVE + Lighthouse; log all issues. 2. Keyboard navigation (1 hr): full Tab pass; all interactions; custom components. 3. Screen reader (1-2 hr): NVDA+Firefox (or JAWS+Chrome); headings, landmarks, all forms, dynamic content, custom widgets. 4. Mobile SR (30 min): VoiceOver on iOS Safari; TalkBack on Android Chrome; test gestures; test forms.

section.name: agentic-pipeline

rule.statement: Drive AT testing as a tiered chain: Playwright+axe for tree coverage, Guidepup for real-speech critical flows, human tester for final sign-off on custom widgets.
rule.rationale: Automation keeps regressions cheap; it cannot replace an expert AT user for nuanced judgment on custom components, live regions, and mobile gestures.

section.reference: Real-speech Playwright + Guidepup test for NVDA form-error announcement
Gherkin template for AT test cases anchored to WCAG SC

list.item: Playwright agent runs axe-core for tree-level coverage on every PR.
Guidepup agent drives NVDA/VoiceOver for curated critical flows (form submit, modal, SR navigation).
Human AT user signs off on custom widgets, live-region semantics, mobile gesture coverage.

antipattern.description: Asserting exact SR speech phrases such as "screen reader will say 'invalid'".
antipattern.reason: Over-specifies phrasing; NVDA and JAWS render the same ARIA differently. Assert on intent (error announced within 1s) not exact string.

antipattern.description: Closing an a11y bug without linking to the WCAG criterion and pre/post screenshot.
antipattern.reason: Reviewers cannot audit drift; regressions are silently reintroduced.

rule.statement: Treat focus restoration after modal close as its own checklist item; it is the most common AT regression.
rule.rationale: Frameworks re-render on modal close and reset focus to document.body; must explicitly return focus to the trigger element.

list.item: Anchor every AT test to a specific WCAG 2.2 Success Criterion — easier to triage and to defend in audit.
Test pairs that real users actually use: NVDA+Firefox, JAWS+Chrome, VoiceOver+Safari, TalkBack+Chrome.
Run keyboard-only and screen-reader passes separately — different bugs surface in each.
Maintain a "known false positive" list; axe and pa11y misfire and noise destroys trust.
Keep one small, fast AT smoke suite in PR CI; full run nightly or pre-release.
Recruit at least one paid AT user for quarterly review — automation will not catch what a habitual user catches.
Treat focus restoration after modal close as its own checklist line item; it's the most common regression.
Agents conflate accessibility-tree probes with real speech output — call out which mode each test uses.
LLM-generated AT test cases assume single-page app behavior; multi-page navigation breaks focus expectations.
Generated assertions like "screen reader will say 'invalid'" overspecify — assert on intent, not exact phrasing.
Mobile AT gesture sequences hallucinate; copy from official Apple/Google docs.
Agents miss the difference between aria-live="polite" vs assertive for error timing — require explicit choice and justification.
tabindex="0" on non-interactive divs is suggested too easily — escalate to human.
Human-in-loop checkpoints: every custom-widget pattern (combobox, tree, grid), live-region semantics, modal/dialog focus trap, mobile gesture coverage, final pre-release suite.
Screen-reader output is not directly capturable from headless browsers; most automation cheats by reading the accessibility tree, not the speech buffer.
JAWS, NVDA, VoiceOver, TalkBack, Orca behave differently for the same DOM — testing one is not testing all.
AT + browser pairings matter (NVDA + Firefox vs. NVDA + Chrome); a green run on one combo does not generalize.
Mobile AT (VoiceOver iOS, TalkBack) gestures are model- and OS-version-specific; flaky in CI.
Live-region timing is non-deterministic — agents fail to assert announcements reliably without polling.

template.purpose: Playwright + Guidepup test asserting inline form error announced by NVDA.
Gherkin template for authoring AT test cases per WCAG SC.
